Titulo: Development Of An Algorithm For Strength Training With Use Haptic Feedback And Artificial Intelligence (AI) Based Enhancement
Autor_norm: E. A. Villegas-Jiménez; A. H. Bello-Guerrero; M. D. Padilla-Torres; L. N. Villagómez-Venegas; C. S. Chávez-Ramírez
Afiliacion1: Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México
Keywords: AI;virtual coach;strength training;biomechanics;haptic feedback;python
Abstract: The increasing popularity of the use of AI in everyday life has enabled the development of virtual systems for human assistance. This research proposes an implemented linear regression model with a vector of random weights, using a mean square error (MSE), based cost function and regularisation and corresponding Python code that integrates AI-based haptic feedback through vibration, specifically designed to optimise weight training.The results of the simulation model analysis show how key biomechanical parameters are identified and used to improve a person's posture by adjusting technique through real-time vibrational stimuli. The development of the system involves physical benefits such as injury prevention, continuous biomechanical monitoring, adaptive programme structuring and haptic vibration feedback for immediate movement correction. Popular and accurate tools for building linguistic models are mentioned, such as machine learning, computer vision and neural networks. Technological tools such as machine learning, computer vision and neural networks, applied to process biomechanical data and trigger personalised vibrational responses, are highlighted.

Titulo: Pseudo-Temporal Modeling with 2D CNNs for Framewise Surgical Gesture Segmentation in Robot-Assisted Surgery
Autor_norm: D. Haro-Mendoza; G. A. Castro Vazquez; A. Leonardo Campos Carreras; A. J. Diaz Urias; V. J. Gonzalez-Villela; M. Cedillo-Hernandez; R. E. Arevalo-Ancona
Afiliacion1: Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Biomedical Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacán, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacán, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, Mexico
Keywords: surgical gesture segmentation;pseudo-temporal modeling;2d convolutional neural networks;resnet 50;exponential moving average
Abstract: Surgical procedures can be decomposed into elementary units (tasks, surgemes, and dexemes) that represent the building blocks of surgical performance. Segmenting gestures at this level is crucial for objective skill assessment and for understanding how simple movements are concatenated to form complete procedures, ultimately supporting the development of training and evaluation systems. In this work, we present a pseudo-temporal modeling approach for surgical gesture segmentation using a 2D ResNet-50 convolutional network trained exclusively on RGB frames. During training, each video was divided into eight equal segments, which were used to feed the network and generate gesture-level probabilities. To optimize learning, an adaptive Exponential Moving Average (EMA) mechanism was applied to stabilize weight updates and reinforce poorly performing gesture classes. At inference, complete surgical videos were processed with an overlapping sliding window of eight frames, where each frame could be evaluated multiple times in different contexts. Frame-level probabilities were averaged across overlapping windows and refined through a temporal mode filter, producing stable and coherent gesture sequences. The method was validated on the JIGSAWS dataset, which includes the Suturing, Knot Tying, and Needle Passing tasks. Results showed reliable recognition across all procedures: Suturing achieved 82.26%, Knot Tying 91.97%, and Needle Passing 79.35%. Training required approximately one hour, and inference on a complete video of 2950 frames was performed in about 45 seconds, also generating a labeled output video. By relying solely on 2D convolutions and RGB input, the approach achieves accurate and efficient segmentation with low computational cost, making it suitable for surgical training environments with limited resources and applicable in near real-time scenarios.

Titulo: Deep Learning Approach for Aortic Valve Localization, Detection and Segmentation in Computed Tomography Volumes
Autor_norm: R. O. Azuara-Domínguez; J. Olveres; B. Escalante-Ramírez; E. Vallejo
Afiliacion1: Posgrado de Ingeniería Eléctrica, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Centro Médico ABC, Mexico city, Mexico
Keywords: 3D volume Cardiac CT;Deep Learning;Regression;Segmentation;Rotation matrix;Attention
Abstract: Standard view cardiac computed tomography (CT) images are of great importance in clinical practice due to the valuable diagnostic information they provide. However, navigating these (3D) medical images to identify such a view can represent a time-consuming process, subject to inter-operator variability. This study presents an approach using a Multiscale Vision Transformer (MViT) to infer the rotation parameters required to obtain standard views and subsequently focus on extracting the left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) view. Once this view is obtained, the aortic valve is detected and segmented using well-known convolutional neural networks (CNNs), specifically ResNet18 for detection and U-Net for segmentation. The methodology used for its training is detailed, along with the metrics employed to evaluate their performance. Finally, the results obtained for each stage are presented. This approach provides a clear understanding of the performance of each model, from rotation parameter prediction to aortic valve detection and segmentation.

Titulo: A Reasoning System for Graded Modal Logic
Autor_norm: R. López-Villafán; E. Bárcenas
Afiliacion1: IIMAS: Posgrado en Ciencia e lngeniería de la Computación, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingenería: Dpto. de Computación, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico
Keywords: graded modalities;proof theory;hypersequents;automated reasoning
Abstract: In this paper, we propose a deduction system for the modal logic $\mathbf{K}$ with graded modalities, which is obtained by adding numerical constraints to the modal operators. Graded modalities can be seen as a generalization of traditional modal operators since they include numerical bounds in the modalities, thus enabling the formal treatment of more detailed expressions, such as “there are at least $n+1$ individuals satisfying a certain property”, where $n$ is a natural number. This kind of properties commonly arise in contexts such as concurrent systems: “if at least 3 processors are free, then proceed to compute the next task”, or epistemic logic:”if more than 2 people know $P$, then $P$ is known”. The system is based on the notion of tree-hypersequents. We show the system is correct, i.e. sound and complete.

Titulo: Large-Scale Validation and Analysis of the Rejection Capacity in Entropic Associative Memory
Autor_norm: Á. F. Bórquez; R. Morales; L. A. Pineda
Afiliacion1: Universidad de Sonora, Guadalajara, Jalisco, México; Universidad de Guadalajara, Guadalajara, Jalisco, México; Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Guadalajara, Jalisco, México
Keywords: Entropic Associative Memory;Quick Draw;scalability;open-set recognition;rejection
Abstract: The Entropic Associative Memory (EAM) is a cognitively inspired computational model that combines probabilistic recognition with an efficient pattern storage and retrieval architecture. Previous evaluations, limited to small domains of about ten classes and a few thousand instances per class, showed promising results in classification and reconstruction. This work extends those studies in two ways. First, we assess scalability using Googles Quick, Draw! dataset, which includes over 300 classes and 100,000 instances per class. Second, we test EAMs rejection capability by storing only half of the classes and evaluating performance on the full dataset. Results confirm EAMs adaptability to large-scale scenarios and its effective rejection of novel stimuli, underscoring its potential as a robust and explainable AI model.

Titulo: A Framework for Dynamic User Modeling Integrating Data Stream Mining and Process Mining in Educational Contexts
Autor_norm: M. Yesenia Zavaleta-Sanchez; E. Benitez-Guerrero; G. Gilberto Molero-Castillo; C. Mezura-Godoy; L. Gerardo Montane-Jimenez
Afiliacion1: Facultad de Estadística e Informática, Universidad Veracruzana (UV), Xalapa, Heroica Veracruz, Mexico; Facultad de Estadística e Informática, Universidad Veracruzana (UV), Xalapa, Heroica Veracruz, Mexico; Departamento de Computación, Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Estadística e Informática, Universidad Veracruzana (UV), Xalapa, Heroica Veracruz, Mexico; Facultad de Estadística e Informática, Universidad Veracruzana (UV), Xalapa, Heroica Veracruz, Mexico
Keywords: Clustering data stream mining;dynamic user modeling;educational data mining;learning styles;process mining
Abstract: A user model (UM) is a representation of the characteristics, behaviors, and preferences of a user that a computer system utilizes to provide the user with personalized experiences. In educational settings, for instance, a system can use information about the Learning Styles (LSs) of a student to provide them with adequate study materials. Furthermore, with the growing use of Learning Management System (LMS) platforms, such as Moodle, continuous streams of user data highlight the need to compute student models dynamically to support adaptive decision-making in teaching and learning. This paper introduces a framework for Dynamic User Modeling (DUMING) to create evolving Dynamic User Models (DUMs) that capture and adapt to changes in student LSs over time, based on event log streams from an LMS. This framework leverages the Felder and Silverman Learning Styles Model (FSLSM) and integrates Data Stream Mining (DSM) and Process Mining (PM) techniques across three layers: collection, preprocessing, and mining. Collected event streams are processed within landmark windows and classified into High-Level Events (HLEs) based on LMS functionality. The STREAMKMeans algorithm clusters events, and the PM phase uses Alpha Miner (AM), Heuristic Miner (HM), and Inductive Miner (IM) algorithms to identify learning paths represented as Petri Nets (PNs), with IM showing the highest values in the quality metrics used. Implemented and evaluated in a course using Moodle at the Universidad Veracruzana in Mexico, the framework employs IF-THEN rules to relate HLE proportions to FSLSM-based LSs, visualizing changes through radar charts. The results demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework in generating adaptive DUMs, providing insights that enhance over time educational support and enable timely interventions.

Titulo: Detection of DoS and DDoS Attacks Using LSTM in Computer Networks: A Systematic Literature Review
Autor_norm: A. E. Godínez; J. L. Garza Rivera; D. T. Varela
Afiliacion1: CDMX, Universidad Da Vinci, México; CDMX, Universidad Da Vinci, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores Cuautitlán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Cuautitlan Izcalli, México
Keywords: Long short term memory;Systematic literature review;Machine learning;Denial-of-service attack;Network security
Abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI), with machine learning (ML) models and deep learning (DL) neural networks, represents a possibility for detecting DoS/DDoS attacks more effectively. The LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) model is one of the options for detecting these increasingly varied and sophisticated attacks. The objective of this research was to evaluate the application of the LSTM model in the detection of DoS and DDoS cyber attacks on communications networks. This research was designed as a Systematic Literature Review to obtain the most recent studies on the subject. As a result, 86 initial studies were obtained from the databases. Once the entire procedure, including the quality assessment of the studies, was completed, 15 final primary studies were selected. With these primary studies, it was possible to answer all the questions in the review. It should be noted that modern SDN (Software Defined Network) networks, Cloud Computing networks, and classic networks are threatened by this type of attack. The LSTM models applied in the detection of DoS/DDoS attacks can be hybrid or combined. The most commonly used evaluation metrics are Accuracy, Recall, Precision, and F1-Score, and the most frequently found public datasets include NSL-KDD, CICDoS2019, and CICIDS2017. A wide variety of LSTM-based models and approaches that can be used to experiment and compare their performance in terms of communications network security.

Titulo: A Parallel Steganography Algorithm Using a New Secret Key Generation
Autor_norm: A. I. H. Al-Jarah; J. L. O. Arjona
Afiliacion1: Postgraduate in Science and Computing Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Mathematics department, Science faculty National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico
Keywords: Hiding data;Image steganography;Processing time improvement;Parallel steganography;Secret key steganography;Steganography
Abstract: Computation has mostly used parallelism for high-performance computation, and multicore processors have gained popularity because they offer better processing time. However, one of the most challenging tasks to achieve is securely exchange confidential data over the network between sender and receiver. The research work in this paper explains how secret key steganography, combined with parallel processing, is utilized to conceal secret data within an image. The proposed algorithm describes and focuses on the generation method of a secret key while merging it with the Least Significant Bit (LSB) algorithm to achieve robustness, as well as parallel computing to improve the processing time in both the encoding/decoding phases. The proposed algorithm adds another layer of security by adding a robustness parameter (secret key), convenient for keeping secret, confidential, and crucial data. Parallel processing, combined with the new method to formulate the secret key is the novelty and strongest attribute of the proposed algorithm. When parallel computing is applied, the process speeds up compared with the same algorithm in the sequential version. The results are compared with the LSB algorithm in terms of the peak signal to noise ratio (PSNR) and mean square error (MSE) values, aspiring to present that the proposed algorithm adds robustness without affecting image imperceptibility.

Titulo: Optimization of Deep Learning Architectures for Accurate Prediction of Protein-Ligand Binding in Drug Discovery
Autor_norm: J. M. Hernández Duarte; A. Y. Alarcón López; E. R. Ángeles Anguiano
Afiliacion1: Universidad Da Vinci, Ciudad de México, Mexico; Departamento de Ciencias Quimicas, FES Cuautitlán - UNAM, Cuautitlán Izcalli, Mexico; Departamento de Ciencias Quimicas, FES Cuautitlán - UNAM, Cuautitlán Izcalli, Mexico
Keywords: Drug discovery;Computational chemistry;Deep learning;Attention mechanisms;Quantum computing
Abstract: Drug discovery is an intricate and costly process, in which the accurate prediction of protein-ligand binding affinity (BAP) represents a critical step. This systematic review article addresses the state of the art in this field, focusing on the evolution and optimization of deep learning (DL) architectures that have emerged as a robust alternative to traditional computational and experimental methods. A taxonomy is proposed that classifies DL models based on their data representation: structure-based models (such as convolutional and graph neural networks), sequence-based models, and hybrid and multimodal approaches. Comparative analysis highlights the superiority of architectures that integrate multimodal information and employ attention mechanisms, demonstrating improved predictive accuracy and generalization capabilities. Nonetheless, there are still issues, such as the limited interpretability of the model or the bias and scarcity of training data. Finally, future research directions emphasize the revolutionary potential of quantum computing, which promises to overcome computational and data limitations, and generative artificial intelligence, which enables de novo molecular design. This work directs the scientific community to the most promising research areas by offering a conceptual framework for comprehending the state of DL-driven BAP today.

Titulo: Data-Driven Power System Linear Model Identification via Quadrature-Based Balanced Truncation
Autor_norm: M. R. Arrieta Paternina; J. A. Moreno Corbea; J. M. Ramirez-Arredondo; J. H. Chow; A. Zamora-Mendez
Afiliacion1: Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico (UNAM), MX; Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico (UNAM), MX; Centro de Investigacion y de Estudios Avanzados del IPN, Unidad Guadalajara, MX; Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute, US; Universidad Michoacana San Nicolas de Hidalgo, MX
Keywords: balanced truncation;quadrature rules;system identification;directional frequency data-set;power system linear model
Abstract: This paper introduces the novel formulation of the quadrature-based balanced truncation (QBBT) method to precisely identify large-scale power system linear models. The QBBT interpolates a directional frequency dataset that is extracted from a dynamic system, following a quadrature rule to be applied from weighted data to derive a linear system model based on approximations of Gramians, and providing an alternative approach to the classic balanced truncation (BT) formulation that makes use of the system model. In this investigation, an accurate theoretical framework is presented to achieve the application of QBBT considering power systems as black-box models. The Boyd/Clenshaw-Curtis (BCC) quadrature rule is implemented by using different adjustments to accurately identify linear models of different order. The attained results and their validation with analytical power system models confirm the method's potential to retail oscillatory dynamics. The QBBT's effectiveness is compared with the Loewner-based frequency interpolation (LBFI) approach and the BT method on the Klein-Rogers-Kundur (KRK) benchmark power system and the equivalent of the New England transmission system - New York power system (NETS-NYPS), achieving a small absolute error in the magnitude of the frequency response of the models of the order of 1x10-3 dB in comparison with other state-of-the-art methods.

Titulo: Sparse Dynamic Network Reconstruction Through L1-regularization of a Lyapunov Equation
Autor_norm: I. X. Belaustegui; M. O. Arango; R. Rossi-Pool; N. E. Leonard; A. Franci
Afiliacion1: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Princeton University; Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Princeton University; Instituto de Fisiología Celular - Neurociencias and Centro de Ciencias de la Complejidad, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, México; Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering, Princeton University; Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science, University of Liege, and WEL Research Institute, Wavre, Belgium
Keywords: Correlation;Reconstruction algorithms;Entropy;Sparse matrices;Noise measurement;Covariance matrices;Dynamical systems
Abstract: An important problem in many areas of science is that of recovering interaction networks from high-dimensional time-series of many interacting dynamical processes. A common approach is to use the elements of the correlation matrix or its inverse as proxies of the interaction strengths, but the reconstructed networks are necessarily undirected. Transfer entropy methods have been proposed to reconstruct directed networks, but the reconstructed network lacks information about interaction strengths. We propose a network reconstruction method that inherits the best of the two approaches by reconstructing a directed weighted network from noisy data under the assumption that the network is sparse and the dynamics are governed by a linear (or weakly-nonlinear) stochastic dynamical system. The two steps of our method are i) constructing an (infinite) family of candidate networks by solving the covariance matrix Lyapunov equation for the state matrix and ii) using $L_{1}$-regularization to select a sparse solution. We further show how to use prior information on the (non)existence of a few directed edges to dramatically improve the quality of the reconstruction.

Titulo: Tool-Assisted Annotation of Seafloor Sediment-Linked Features Using Weakly Supervised Semantic Segmentation
Autor_norm: S. Heidari; M. Rogers; J. Wang; D. A. S. Valdez; S. Schenone; G. Cunnington; P. Delmas; S. Thrush
Afiliacion1: Institute of Marine Science, The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; Centre for Data Science and Artificial Intelligence, Victoria University of Wellington, Wellington, New Zealand; Intelligent Vision Systems Lab (IVSLab), The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; ICAT, UNAM, Mexico City, Mexico; Institute of Marine Science, The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; Institute of Marine Science, The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; Intelligent Vision Systems Lab (IVSLab), The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand; Institute of Marine Science, The University of Auckland, Auckland, New Zealand
Keywords: weakly supervised learning;semantic segmentation;few-shot learning;feature enhancement;self-supervised learning;seafloor sediment
Abstract: Pixel-wise labeling of seafloor imagery is highly time-consuming, limiting the scalability of benthic habitat monitoring. While existing underwater computer vision research has largely focused on visually prominent habitats, sediment-linked benthic features remain underexplored and lack annotated datasets. To address this gap, we propose a tool-assisted annotation framework based on weakly supervised semantic segmentation. The framework follows a three-phase pipeline: feature-based pseudo-mask generation, binary classspecific segmentation, and iterative multiclass segmentation with pseudo-mask expansion and affinity-field regularization. Applied to six ecologically important sediment features, the approach progressively improves pseudo-label quality while reducing reliance on dense expert annotations, providing a scalable solution that can accelerate the annotation of seafloor sediment features. Code is available at: https://github.com/shahrokh1106/seafloor-sediment-segmentation

Titulo: Real-Time Probabilistic Reachability Forecasting for Gliders in the Gulf of Mexico
Autor_norm: E. M. Mule; P. J. Haley; C. Mirabito; S. F. DiMarco; S. Mahmud; A. Dancer; X. Ge; A. H. Knap; Y. Liu; U. C. Nwankwo; S. Glenn; T. N. Miles; D. Aragon; K. Coleman; M. Smith; M. Leber; R. Ramos; J. Storie; G. Stuart; J. Marble; P. Barros; E. P. Chassignet; A. Bower; H. H. Furey; B. J. De La Cruz; L. K. Shay; M. Tenreiro; E. P. Sanz; J. Sheinbaum; P. Perez-Brunius; D. Wilson; J. Van Smirren; R. Monreal-Jiménez; D. A. Salas-de-León; V. K. C. Tereza; M. Feldman; M. Khadka; P. F. J. Lermusiaux
Afiliacion1: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA; Department of Mechanical Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA; Department of Mechanical Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA; Department of Oceanography, Texas A&M University; Department of Oceanography, Texas A&M University; Department of Oceanography, Texas A&M University; Department of Oceanography, Texas A&M University; Department of Oceanography, Texas A&M University; Department of Oceanography, Texas A&M University; Department of Oceanography, Texas A&M University; Department of Marine and Coastal Sciences, Rutgers University; Department of Marine and Coastal Sciences, Rutgers University; Department of Marine and Coastal Sciences, Rutgers University; Department of Marine and Coastal Sciences, Rutgers University; Department of Marine and Coastal Sciences, Rutgers University; Woods Hole Group, Inc.; Woods Hole Group, Inc.; Woods Hole Group, Inc.; Fugro; Fugro; Fugro; Department of Earth, Ocean and Atmospheric Science, Florida State University; Physical Oceanography, Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution; Physical Oceanography, Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution; Rosenstiel School of Marine, Atmospheric, and Earth Science, University of Miami; Rosenstiel School of Marine, Atmospheric, and Earth Science, University of Miami; Department of Oceanography, CICESE; Department of Oceanography, CICESE; Department of Oceanography, CICESE; Department of Oceanography, CICESE; University of the Virgin Islands; Ocean Sierra; Universidad Nacional Autónoma de Mexico; Universidad Nacional Autónoma de Mexico; Mexican Institute of Water Technology; Gulf Research Program, NASEM; Gulf Research Program, NASEM; Department of Mechanical Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA
Keywords: Ocean forecasting;sea glider;path planning;reachability analysis;probabilistic forecasting;adaptive sampling
Abstract: As part of the Mini-Adaptive Sampling Test Run (MASTR) experiment in the Gulf of Mexico (GoM) region from February to April 2024, we demonstrated real-time deterministic and probabilistic reachability analysis and time-optimal path planning to guide a fleet of four ocean gliders. The governing differential equations for reachability analysis and time-optimal path planning were numerically integrated in real-time and forced by currents from our large-ensemble ocean forecasts. We illustrate the real-time deterministic and probabilistic forward reachability analyses, reachability and path planning for glider pickups, time-optimal path planning for gliders in distress, and planning of future glider deployments. Results show that the actual paths of gliders were contained within our reachable set forecasts and in accord with the dynamic reachability fronts. Our time-optimal headings and paths also predicted real glider motions, even for longer-range predictions of weeks to a month duration. Reachability and time-optimal path planning forecasts were successfully employed for glider recovery. They also enabled exploring options for future glider deployments.

Titulo: Multi-Machine Power System Simulation via Differential Transformation
Autor_norm: R. Galarza-Villa; A. Zamora-Mendez; C. Perez-Rojas; M. R. Arrieta Paternina
Afiliacion1: Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo (UMSNH), MX; Universidad Michoacana San Nicolas de Hidalgo, MX; Universidad Michoacana de San Nicolas de Hidalgo (UMSNH), MX; Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico (UNAM), MX
Keywords: differential transformation;multimachine simulation;transient stability;numerical integration;power system toolbox
Abstract: In this paper, the differential transformation (DT) is applied to the dynamic analysis of multi-machine systems, whose solution is numerically obtained by solving a set of differential-algebraic equations that represent the power system dynamics. This method is an algorithm to calculate the Taylor coefficients in a fast and efficient way, making the Taylor method competitive against traditional numerical integration methods such as the modified Euler (ME) method and trapezoidal rule (TR). Thus, the DT's rules for solving differential-algebraic equations of large multi-machine power systems are applied. To exhibit the DT's performance, the power system toolbox (PST) is adopted and compared against other integration methods for multi-machine power system simulation. Finally, time-domain simulations on the WECC and Mexican systems are performed by means of DT and compared with ME and TR methods to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposal, showcasing that our proposal improves up to 12 times the traditional methods.

Titulo: Consistent IoT Systems: A Perspective Based on Paraconsistent Descriptive Logic
Autor_norm: M. Pérez-Gaspar; J. Gomez; E. Bárcenas; F. García
Afiliacion1: Dept. of Telecommunications, UNAM, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Telecommunications, UNAM, Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Computer Engineering, UNAM, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Telecommunications, UNAM, Mexico City, Mexico
Keywords: Internet of Things;data inconsistency;paracon-sistent logic;consistency theorem
Abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) devices generate vast and often contradictory data streams. This poses a challenge for IoT systems, which must make accurate and reliable decisions based on this data. Paraconsistent descriptive logic (PDL) is a branch of non-classical logic that can handle inconsistencies without compromising data reliability. We propose the integration of PDL into IoT systems to improve data coherence and reliability. We also introduce a Consistency Theorem that establishes rules for managing inconsistencies systematically and in a controlled way. Our efforts aim to pave the way for more robust, reliable, and user-friendly IoT systems.

Titulo: Invited Paper: Further Decentralizing Decentralized Finance: Loss vs Rebalancing Without a Shared Reference Market
Autor_norm: M. Herlihy; S. Rajsbaum
Afiliacion1: Computer Science Department, Brown University, Providence, USA; Instituto de Matemáticas UNAM, Mexico City, Mexico
Keywords: component;formatting;style;styling;insert
Abstract: Automated market makers (AMMs) typically rely on arbitrage agents to keep prices in line with a shared reference market such as a large centralized exchange. This paper considers an alternative, even-more-decentralized model where prices must stabilize without a shared reference market.We first consider a model where there is one population of AMMs, and another of arbitrage agents who seek to profit from pairwise price differences between randomly-chosen AMMs. For constant-product AMMs, repeated random pair-wise arbitrage causes the AMMs’ expected prices to converge within any precision ϵ > 0 in $\Theta \left( {\max \left( {{n^2}\log n,\log \frac{1}{\varepsilon }} \right)} \right.$ interactions, and arbitrage agents’ profits are proportional to the original price imbalances. Within certain limits, the arbitrage agents can collude to set the final stable price.If, instead, randomly-chosen pairs of AMMs could rebalance their asset pools directly, capturing profits that would have gone to arbitrage agents, then expected AMM prices converge within ϵ with respective upper and lower bounds of $\Omega \left( {\max \left( {{n^2}\log n,\log \frac{1}{\varepsilon }} \right)} \right.$ and $O\left( {\max \left( {{n^2}\log n,\log \frac{1}{\varepsilon }} \right)} \right.$ interactions. Within certain limits, the AMMs can collude to set the final stable price.

Titulo: In-Silico Trained AI for Enhanced T2 Spectrum Imaging and Myelin Water Fraction Mapping in Preclinical 7T MRI
Autor_norm: D. Vallejo-Aldana; A. Ramírez-Manzanares; E. J. Canales-Rodríguez; T. Yu; A. Cisneros-Mejorado; L. Concha; J. Rafael-Patiño; J. -P. Thiran
Afiliacion1: Computer Science Department, Centro de Investigación en Matemáticas A.C, Guanajuato, México; Computer Science Department, Centro de Investigación en Matemáticas A.C, Guanajuato, México; Signal Processing Laboratory (LTS5), École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL), Lausanne, Switzerland; Signal Processing Laboratory (LTS5), École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL), Lausanne, Switzerland; Instituto de Neurobiología, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Juriquilla, México; Instituto de Neurobiología, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Juriquilla, México; Signal Processing Laboratory (LTS5), École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL), Lausanne, Switzerland; Signal Processing Laboratory (LTS5), École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL), Lausanne, Switzerland
Keywords: In-silico;Machine learning;Brain;MRI;Myelin water imaging;Pre-clinical data.
Abstract: This study introduces a Machine Learning (ML) approach for estimating the T2 spectrum and myelin water fraction (MWF) using multi-echo T2 (MET2) data from preclinical 7T Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) scanners. ML methods have shown promise in MWF estimation, outperforming Regularized Non-Negative Least Squares (RNNLS). However, existing ML methods were optimized for high signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) typical of 3T clinical MET2 data with larger voxel sizes. We adapted the Model-Informed Machine Learning (MIML) method to handle challenges in preclinical 7T MRI, including reduced voxel sizes, elevated noise levels (SNR=30-60), and shifts in T2 lobes. Results from in-silico simulated data demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed multi-layer-perceptron-based solution over RNNLS. Validation with ME T2 data from two mice—a healthy control and a cuprizone-exposed pathological mouse—confirms the ML method’s success in identifying cuprizone-induced demyelination. Our study showcases the adaptability and enhanced performance of the MIML approach under challenging preclinical 7T MRI conditions, contributing to the advancement of MWF estimation methods in high-field MRI settings.

Titulo: Mitigating Limited Angle Artifacts in a Dual-Panel Positron Emission Mammography Scanner Using Deep Learning Regularization
Autor_norm: F. G. Moncada-Gutiérrez; M. Rodríguez-Villafuerte; H. Alva-Sánchez; A. Martínez-Dávalos
Afiliacion1: National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico City, Mexico; National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico City, Mexico; National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico City, Mexico; National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico City, Mexico
Keywords: Deep learning;Training;Microwave integrated circuits;Sensitivity;Monte Carlo methods;Semiconductor detectors;Positrons
Abstract: A dual-panel Positron Emission Mammography (PEM) scanner is a dedicated breast imaging device with higher spatial resolution and enhanced sensitivity, in comparison with conventional Positron Emission Tomography (PET) scanners. However, one of the main challenges this scanner faces are the limited angle artifacts that arise in the cross-planes. To mitigate such artifacts, deep learning regularization has been included in the List Mode Ordered Subsets Expectation Maximization (LM-OSEM) reconstruction algorithm. Regularization has been applied using a U-Net trained with Monte-Carlo simulated data. Network training was achieved using slices of cross-plane images of a PEM scanner as input and corresponding slices from a dedicated breast-PET ring scanner as target, since this scanner does not have the limited angle problem. In the regularization stage, the trained U-Net is employed to process the cross-plane slices of the preceding image estimation. Subsequently, the resultant output is fused with the former image estimate utilizing Forward-backward splitting expectation maximization (FBSEM). This image reconstruction framework has demonstrated a notable enhancement in image quality when assessed from the cross-plane perspective, while maintaining the quality of images when assessed from the in-plane perspective. With this approach, PEM images may be effectively analyzed as three-dimensional images.

Titulo: Incorporation of Deep Learning Regularization in the Image Reconstruction Algorithm for a Dual-Panel PEM Scanner
Autor_norm: F. G. Moncada-Gutiérrez; M. Rodríguez-Villafuerte; H. Alva-Sánchez; A. Martínez-Dávalos
Afiliacion1: CDMX, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico; CDMX, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico; CDMX, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico; CDMX, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico
Keywords: Image reconstruction;Deep learning;Positrons;Physics;Plasmas;Nuclear medicine;Image quality;Distortion;Training data;Single photon emission computed tomography
Abstract: A dual-panel Positron Emission Mammography (PEM) scanner prototype is under development in our research group, and its imaging performance is currently being evaluated. The main challenge for dual-panel scanners is the presence of limited-angle artifacts, which appear in the images due to the acquisition geometry. These artifacts diminish the quality of the images, degrade the contrast of the lesions, and generate distortions in the cross-plane slices (perpendicular to the panels). To mitigate such distortions, deep learning regularization has been incorporated into the List Mode Ordered Subsets Expectation Maximization (LM-OSEM) image reconstruction algorithm. For the regularization step, a U-Net was trained with Monte-Carlo simulated data for several image quality phantoms. Data augmentation strategies were used to increase the diversity of the training data and to construct a model that generalizes more effectively to unseen data. The deep learning model was trained with 3840 pairs of cross-plane images of a dual-panel PEM scanner as input and corresponding slices from a dedicated breast-PET ring scanner as target. This approach was selected because a ring-shaped scanner does not face the limited-angle problem. The Forward-backward splitting expectation maximization (FBSEM) algorithm was implemented with the trained U-Net. This framework was applied to both MC-simulated and experimentally acquired data. In both cases, the quality of the cross-plane images was enhanced, without hampering the in-plane slices (parallel to the panels), and the enhancement in the quality of the images was quantified by image quality metrics like noise, contrast, and the Mean Absolute Error (MAE).

Titulo: Finding ϵ-Locally Optimal Solutions for Multiobjective Multimodal Optimization
Autor_norm: A. E. Rodriguez-Fernandez; L. Schäpermeier; C. Hernández; P. Kerschke; H. Trautmann; O. Schütze
Afiliacion1: Department of Computer Science, CINVESTAV-IPN, Ciudad de Mexico, Mexico; Big Data Analytics in Transportation, Technische Universitat Dresde, Dresden, Germany; Ciencias de la computación, Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico Instituto de Investigaciones en Matematicas Aplicadas y Sistemas, Ciudad de Mexico, Mexico; Big Data Analytics in Transportation, Technische Universitat Dresde, Dresden, Germany; Department of Machine Learning and Optimisation, University of Paderborn, Paderborn, Germany; Department of Computer Science, CINVESTAV-IPN, Ciudad de Mexico, Mexico
Keywords: Evolutionary computation;local solutions;multimodal optimization (MMO);multiobjective optimization
Abstract: In this article, we address the problem of computing all locally optimal solutions of a given multiobjective problem whose images are sufficiently close to the Pareto front. Such $\epsilon $ -locally optimal solutions are particularly interesting in the context of multiobjective multimodal optimization (MMO). To accomplish this task, we first define a new set of interest, $L_{Q,\epsilon }$ , that is strongly related to the recently proposed set of $\epsilon $ -acceptable solutions. Next, we propose a new unbounded archiver, $ArchiveUpdateL_{Q,\epsilon }$ , aiming to capture $L_{Q,\epsilon }$ in the limit. This archiver can in principle be used in combination with any multiobjective evolutionary algorithm (MOEA). Further, we equip numerous MOEAs with $ArchiveUpdateL_{Q,\epsilon }$ , investigate their performances across several benchmark functions, and compare the enhanced MOEAs with their archive-free counterparts. For our experiments, we utilize the well-established metrics HV, IGDX, and $\Delta _{p}$ . Additionally, we propose and use a new performance indicator, $I_{\mathrm { EDR}}$ , which results in comparable performances but which is applicable to problems defined in higher dimensions (in particular in decision variable space).

Titulo: A Finite-Time Convergent Primal-Dual Gradient Dynamics Based on the Multivariable Super-Twisting Algorithm
Autor_norm: O. Texis-Loaiza; A. Mercado-Uribe; J. A. Moreno; J. Schiffer
Afiliacion1: Brandenburg University of Technology Cottbus-Senftenberg, Cottbus, Germany; Brandenburg University of Technology Cottbus-Senftenberg, Cottbus, Germany; Eléctrica y Computación, Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; Brandenburg University of Technology Cottbus-Senftenberg, Cottbus, Germany
Keywords: Heuristic algorithms;Europe;Time factors;Optimization;Standards;Convergence;Lyapunov methods
Abstract: We propose a novel primal-dual gradient dynamics (PDGD) algorithm to dynamically solve an optimization problem with linear equality constraints in finite time. To ensure finite-time convergence, we endow the PDGD with suitable homogeneity properties. More precisely, departing from the standard PDGD and based on the associated Lagrangian of the optimization problem, the algorithm is derived by suitably combining a change of coordinates of the standard PDGD with the multivariable super-twisting algorithm. In our new coordinates, the proposed PDGD’s global convergence to the optimal solution of the optimization problem is then proven via a smooth, strong Lyapunov function. Additionally, we provide a numerical example to compare the performance of our algorithm with existing approaches from the literature.

Titulo: An Evolutionary Approach for the Computation of ∈-Locally Optimal Solutions for Multi-Objective Multimodal Optimization
Autor_norm: C. Hernández; A. E. Rodriguez-Fernandez; L. Schäpermeier; O. Cuate; H. Trautmann; O. Schütze
Afiliacion1: IIMAS-UNAM, Mexico; CINVESTAV-IPN, Mexico; University of Münster, Germany; ESFM -Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico; University of Paderborn, Germany; CINVESTAV-IPN, Mexico
Keywords: Multi-objective optimization;evolutionary algorithms;nearly optimal solutions;multimodal optimization;archiving;continuation
Abstract: In this paper, we address the problem of efficiently computing finite-size approximations of the set of -locally optimal solutions of a given multi-objective optimization problem (MOP). Such sets are in particular interesting in the context of multi-objective multimodal optimization (MMO). To this end, we first propose a bounded archiver, ArchiveUpdateLQ,∈B, that is, a modification of a previously proposed unbounded archiver. These archivers can be used as external archivers to in principle any multi-objective evolutionary algorithm (MOEA). In order to reduce the computational cost compared to such archive equipped MOEAs, we propose, in a next step LQ,∈MOEA. This evolutionary algorithm directly uses ArchiveUpdateLQ,∈B for the selection process and hence does not need an external archive for the computation of ∈-locally optimal solutions. We further propose a hybrid of LQ,∈MOEA with a multi-objective continuation method, which significantly improves the accuracy of the obtained solutions in case the gradient information is at hand. Finally, we show some numerical results that demonstrate the benefit of both the bounded archiver and the new MOEAs.

Titulo: Protection Of Sensitive Personal Information Contained In Digital Files Held By Individuals With Homomorphic Encryption And AI Assistance
Autor_norm: E. A. Villegas-Jiménez; A. Romo-Cabrera; M. A. Manzano-Calixto; L. M. Tello-Aguilera; A. K. Sánchez-Mata; A. Robles-Guerrero; P. M. Hernández-Ruiz
Afiliacion1: Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México
Keywords: deep fake;encryption;hormomorphic encryption;artificial intelligence;symmetric encryption;asymmetric encryption;multimedia files
Abstract: In today’s digital landscape, the exchange of sensitive personal data via mobile devices is widespread, yet inadequate safeguards often expose users to risks such as identity theft and online harassment. This research introduces Kamplae, a system crafted in Python and deployed through a Flask-based REST API, employing homomorphic encryption and artificial intelligence to protect personal information embedded in digital images. Kamplae secures metadata and bitmap content in jpeg, jpg, and png formats, adhering to legal standards in Mexico and the European Union.The study reviews prior work on personal data protection and the challenges posed by deep fakes, emphasising the need for robust regulatory frameworks to counter digital violence. It explores various encryption methods—symmetric, asymmetric, and hybrid—alongside relevant legal provisions across regions.Kamplae aims to prevent image misuse and foster a secure, ethical digital environment. While promising, it highlights areas for enhancement, such as broader format support and user interface refinement, with limitations tied to specific format compatibility and mobile performance.

Titulo: Development Of An Algorithm For Strength Training With Use Haptic Feedback And Artificial Intelligence (AI) Based Enhancement
Autor_norm: E. A. Villegas-Jiménez; A. H. Bello-Guerrero; M. D. Padilla-Torres; L. N. Villagómez-Venegas; C. S. Chávez-Ramírez
Afiliacion1: Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores "Aragón", Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Estado de México, México
Keywords: AI;virtual coach;strength training;biomechanics;haptic feedback;python
Abstract: The increasing popularity of the use of AI in everyday life has enabled the development of virtual systems for human assistance. This research proposes an implemented linear regression model with a vector of random weights, using a mean square error (MSE), based cost function and regularisation and corresponding Python code that integrates AI-based haptic feedback through vibration, specifically designed to optimise weight training.The results of the simulation model analysis show how key biomechanical parameters are identified and used to improve a person's posture by adjusting technique through real-time vibrational stimuli. The development of the system involves physical benefits such as injury prevention, continuous biomechanical monitoring, adaptive programme structuring and haptic vibration feedback for immediate movement correction. Popular and accurate tools for building linguistic models are mentioned, such as machine learning, computer vision and neural networks. Technological tools such as machine learning, computer vision and neural networks, applied to process biomechanical data and trigger personalised vibrational responses, are highlighted.

Titulo: Pseudo-Temporal Modeling with 2D CNNs for Framewise Surgical Gesture Segmentation in Robot-Assisted Surgery
Autor_norm: D. Haro-Mendoza; G. A. Castro Vazquez; A. Leonardo Campos Carreras; A. J. Diaz Urias; V. J. Gonzalez-Villela; M. Cedillo-Hernandez; R. E. Arevalo-Ancona
Afiliacion1: Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Biomedical Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacán, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacán, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, Mexico
Keywords: surgical gesture segmentation;pseudo-temporal modeling;2d convolutional neural networks;resnet 50;exponential moving average
Abstract: Surgical procedures can be decomposed into elementary units (tasks, surgemes, and dexemes) that represent the building blocks of surgical performance. Segmenting gestures at this level is crucial for objective skill assessment and for understanding how simple movements are concatenated to form complete procedures, ultimately supporting the development of training and evaluation systems. In this work, we present a pseudo-temporal modeling approach for surgical gesture segmentation using a 2D ResNet-50 convolutional network trained exclusively on RGB frames. During training, each video was divided into eight equal segments, which were used to feed the network and generate gesture-level probabilities. To optimize learning, an adaptive Exponential Moving Average (EMA) mechanism was applied to stabilize weight updates and reinforce poorly performing gesture classes. At inference, complete surgical videos were processed with an overlapping sliding window of eight frames, where each frame could be evaluated multiple times in different contexts. Frame-level probabilities were averaged across overlapping windows and refined through a temporal mode filter, producing stable and coherent gesture sequences. The method was validated on the JIGSAWS dataset, which includes the Suturing, Knot Tying, and Needle Passing tasks. Results showed reliable recognition across all procedures: Suturing achieved 82.26%, Knot Tying 91.97%, and Needle Passing 79.35%. Training required approximately one hour, and inference on a complete video of 2950 frames was performed in about 45 seconds, also generating a labeled output video. By relying solely on 2D convolutions and RGB input, the approach achieves accurate and efficient segmentation with low computational cost, making it suitable for surgical training environments with limited resources and applicable in near real-time scenarios.

Titulo: Deep Learning-based Echocardiography Enhancement: Reducing Speckle Noise and Improving Temporal Resolution
Autor_norm: G. Carcedo-Rodríguez; B. Vazquez; J. Perez-Gonzalez; N. Hevia-Montiel
Afiliacion1: Master Program in Computer Science and Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Unidad Académica en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Unidad Académica en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas, Unidad Académica en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, México
Keywords: Heart rate;Training;Interpolation;Ultrasonic imaging;Noise reduction;Pipelines;Noise;Speckle;Spatial resolution;Testing
Abstract: Ultrasound is widely used in medical imaging due to its accessibility and cost-effectiveness. This paper presents a Deep Learning-based approach for enhancing echocardiographic imaging by addressing two challenges: speckle noise reduction and temporal resolution improvement. For denoising task, we propose the SE-U-Net model, while for temporal enhancement we employ Real-Time Intermediate Flow Estimation for frame interpolation. Multiple datasets were used for training, validation, and testing of both models. The proposed pipeline was applied in high heart rate murine models to enhance relevant information. In final evaluation, the denoising model achieved SSIM=0.99 and PSNR=38.27dB, indicating effective contrast enhancement, preservation of homogeneous regions, and reduction of small artifacts. The interpolation model, on the other hand, achieved SSIM=0.87 and PSNR=33.23dB, enabling the increase of ultrasound temporal resolution from 37 to 296 frames per second without losing image quality.

Titulo: Convolutional Neural Network Trained with Heatmaps for Segmentation and Measurement of the Femur in Fetal Ultrasound
Autor_norm: J. C. A. Ortíz; F. A. Cosío; J. Pérez-González; N. O. C. Díaz; R. V. Cristerna; V. M. Bañuelos; L. C. Marín; M. G. Huerta
Afiliacion1: Unidad Académica del Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, México; Unidad Académica del Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, México; Unidad Académica del Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, México; Laboratorio de Investigación en Neuroimagenología, Universidad Autónoma Metropolitana Unidad Iztapalapa, Ciudad de México, México; Laboratorio de Investigación en Neuroimagenología, Universidad Autónoma Metropolitana Unidad Iztapalapa, Ciudad de México, México; Laboratorio de Investigación en Neuroimagenología, Universidad Autónoma Metropolitana Unidad Iztapalapa, Ciudad de México, México; Departamento de Obstetricia, Instituto Nacional de Perinatología, Ciudad de México, México; Departamento de Obstetricia, Instituto Nacional de Perinatología, Ciudad de México, México
Keywords: Heating systems;Ultrasonic imaging;Ultrasonic variables measurement;Measurement uncertainty;Estimation;Medical services;Manuals;Biomedical measurement;Length measurement;Convolutional neural networks
Abstract: Fetal femur length measurement via ultrasound, alongside abdominal circumference, biparietal diameter, and cranial circumference assessments, are key components of prenatal screening, as they enable the estimation of fetal weight and the evaluation of intrauterine development. Currently, this procedure still heavily relies on manual intervention by expert radiologists or sonographers, who must identify anatomical landmarks to perform the measurements. This dependence on specialized human expertise limits diagnostic accessibility in remote areas far from major urban centers. In this work, we propose a novel convolutional neural network trained on binary masks and heatmaps of femur endpoints. The model achieves robust performance, segmenting the femur with an 88% Dice coefficient, and measuring its length with only a 2.515% error margin. This work is part of a broader machine learning based system for fetal weight estimation, aiming to expand diagnostic capabilities to healthcare centers with limited human resources.

Titulo: Smart Machine Vision for Universal Spatial-Mode Reconstruction
Autor_norm: J. D. Huerta-Morales; C. You; O. S. Magaña-Loaiza; S. -H. Dong; R. d. J. León-Montiel; M. A. Quiroz-Juárez
Afiliacion1: Instituto de Ciencias Nucleares, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad de México, Mexico; Department of Physics and Astronomy, Quantum Photonics Laboratory, Louisiana State University, Baton Rouge, LA, USA; Department of Physics and Astronomy, Quantum Photonics Laboratory, Louisiana State University, Baton Rouge, LA, USA; Research Center for Quantum Physics, Huzhou University, Huzhou, China; Instituto de Ciencias Nucleares, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad de México, Mexico; Centro de Física Aplicada y Tecnología Avanzada, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Querétaro, Mexico
Keywords: Artificial intelligence (AI);neural networks;smart machine-vision device;spatial-mode reconstruction
Abstract: Structured light beams, in particular, those carrying orbital angular momentum (OAM), have gained a lot of attention due to their potential for enlarging the transmission capabilities of communication systems. However, the use of OAM-carrying light in communications faces two major problems, namely distortions introduced during propagation in disordered media, such as the atmosphere or optical fibers, and the large divergence that high-order OAM modes experience. While the use of nonorthogonal modes may offer a way to circumvent the divergence of high-order OAM fields, artificial intelligence (AI) algorithms have shown promise for solving the mode-distortion issue. Unfortunately, current AI-based algorithms make use of large-amount data-handling protocols that generally lead to large processing time and high power consumption. Here, we show that a low-power, low-cost image sensor can act as an artificial neural network that simultaneously detects and reconstructs distorted OAM-carrying beams. We demonstrate the capabilities of our device by reconstructing (with a 95% efficiency) individual Vortex, Laguerre-Gaussian (LG), and Bessel modes, as well as hybrid (nonorthogonal) coherent superpositions of such modes. Our work provides a potentially useful basis for the development of low-power-consumption, light-based communication devices.

Titulo: Deep Learning Approach for Aortic Valve Localization, Detection and Segmentation in Computed Tomography Volumes
Autor_norm: R. O. Azuara-Domínguez; J. Olveres; B. Escalante-Ramírez; E. Vallejo
Afiliacion1: Posgrado de Ingeniería Eléctrica, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Centro Médico ABC, Mexico city, Mexico
Keywords: 3D volume Cardiac CT;Deep Learning;Regression;Segmentation;Rotation matrix;Attention
Abstract: Standard view cardiac computed tomography (CT) images are of great importance in clinical practice due to the valuable diagnostic information they provide. However, navigating these (3D) medical images to identify such a view can represent a time-consuming process, subject to inter-operator variability. This study presents an approach using a Multiscale Vision Transformer (MViT) to infer the rotation parameters required to obtain standard views and subsequently focus on extracting the left ventricular outflow tract (LVOT) view. Once this view is obtained, the aortic valve is detected and segmented using well-known convolutional neural networks (CNNs), specifically ResNet18 for detection and U-Net for segmentation. The methodology used for its training is detailed, along with the metrics employed to evaluate their performance. Finally, the results obtained for each stage are presented. This approach provides a clear understanding of the performance of each model, from rotation parameter prediction to aortic valve detection and segmentation.

Titulo: MMDA: A Multimodal and Multisource Domain Adaptation Method for Cross-Subject Emotion Recognition From EEG and Eye Movement Signals
Autor_norm: M. Jiménez-Guarneros; G. Fuentes-Pineda; J. Grande-Barreto
Afiliacion1: Department of Computer Science, Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas (IIMAS), Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Coyoacán, Ciudad de México, Mexico; Department of Computer Science, Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas (IIMAS), Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Coyoacán, Ciudad de México, Mexico; Information Technology Engineering, Universidad Politécnica de Puebla, Cuanalá, Puebla, Mexico
Keywords: Deep learning;electroencephalogram (EEG);eye movement (EM);multimodal emotion recognition (MER);multimodal unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA);multisource domain adaptation (MDA)
Abstract: Multimodal emotion recognition from electroencephalogram (EEG) and eye movement signals has shown to be a promising approach to provide more discriminative information about human emotional states. However, most current works rely on a subject-dependent approach, limiting their applicability to new users. Recently, some studies have explored multimodal domain adaptation to address the mentioned issue by transferring information from known subjects to new ones. Unfortunately, existing methods are still exposed to negative transfer as a suboptimal distribution alignment is performed between subjects, while irrelevant information is not discarded. In this article, we present a multimodal and multisource domain adaptation (MMDA) method, which adopts the following three strategies: 1) marginal and conditional distribution alignments must be performed between each known subject and a new one; 2) relevant distribution alignments must be prioritized to avoid a negative transfer; and 3) modality fusion results should be improved by extracting more discriminative features from EEG signals and selecting relevant features across modalities. Our proposed method was evaluated with leave-one-subject-out cross validation on four public datasets: SEED, SEED-GER, SEED-IV, and SEED-V. Experimental results show that our proposal outperforms state-of-the-art results for each dataset when subject data from different sessions are combined into a single dataset. Moreover, MMDA exceeds the state of the art in 8 out of 11 different sessions when each session is evaluated.

Titulo: Deep learning for Chagas disease: YOLOv8-based automatic detection of amastigote nests
Autor_norm: D. Sosa; B. Vázquez; N. Hevia-Montiel
Afiliacion1: Ingeniería en Robótica Computacional, Universidad Politécnica de Yucatán, Mérida, Yucatán, Mexico; Unidad Académica del Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, Mexico; Unidad Académica del Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas en el Estado de Yucatán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mérida, Yucatán, Mexico
Keywords: Chagas disease;YOLOv8;deep learning;computer vision;object detection;biomedical imaging
Abstract: Chagas disease, caused by the Trypanosoma cruzi parasite, remains a global public health problem, with cases reported even outside its traditional endemic areas. Its primary clinical manifestation occurs at the cardiac level, characterized by the formation of amastigote nests. This work presents a deep learning algorithm for automatic detection of amastigote nests in cardiac histopathological images obtained from a murine model. For the nests’ detection, a YOLOv8-based architecture was trained on histological images performing a 5-fold cross-validation, while the final tests were carried out with data unseen by the YOLOv8 from four image groups of different post-infection days. In training stage, YOLOv8 achieved 97.9% in accuracy, 98.6±0.3% in precision, and 97.2±0.8% in recall, while in the final test, the best result identified was 78.6% in accuracy, 76.4% in precision, and 81.1% in recall on images at 100× magnification. These metrics reveal the model’s ability to generalize well and its high sensitivity to unseen data. The results of this study show that the proposed algorithm is capable of identifying amastigote nests, which may be useful in clinical areas to understand the damage caused by Trypanosoma cruzi infection.

Titulo: Multimodal Semi-Supervised Domain Adaptation Using Cross-Modal Learning and Joint Distribution Alignment for Cross-Subject Emotion Recognition
Autor_norm: M. Jiménez-Guarneros; G. Fuentes-Pineda; J. Grande-Barreto
Afiliacion1: Department of Computer Science, Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas (IIMAS), Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Computer Science, Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas (IIMAS), Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Information Technology Engineering, Universidad Politécnica de Puebla, Puebla, Mexico
Keywords: Deep learning;electroencephalogram (EEG);emotion recognition;eye movement (EM);multimodal semi-supervised domain adaptation (SSDA)
Abstract: Multimodal physiological data from electroencephalogram (EEG) and eye movement (EM) signals have been shown to be useful in effectively recognizing human emotional states. Unfortunately, individual differences reduce the applicability of existing multimodal classifiers to new users, as low performance is usually observed. Indeed, existing works mainly focus on multimodal domain adaptation from a labeled source domain and unlabeled target domain to address the mentioned problem, transferring knowledge from known subjects to new one. However, a limited set of labeled target data has not been effectively exploited to enhance the knowledge transfer between subjects. In this article, we propose a multimodal semi-supervised domain adaptation (SSDA) method, called cross-modal learning and joint distribution alignment (CMJDA), to address the limitations of existing works, following three strategies: 1) discriminative features are exploited per modality through independent neural networks; 2) correlated features and consistent predictions are produced between modalities; and 3) marginal and conditional distributions are encouraged to be similar between the labeled source data, limited labeled target data, and abundant unlabeled target data. We conducted comparison experiments on two public benchmarks for emotion recognition, SEED-IV and SEED-V, using leave-one-out cross-validation (LOOCV). Our proposal achieves an average accuracy of 92.50%–96.13% across the three available sessions on SEED-IV and SEED-V, only including three labeled target samples per class from the first recorded trial.

Titulo: CFDA-CSF: A Multi-Modal Domain Adaptation Method for Cross-Subject Emotion Recognition
Autor_norm: M. Jiménez-Guarneros; G. Fuentes-Pineda
Afiliacion1: Department of Computer Science, Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas (IIMAS), Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Coyoacán, Mexico; Department of Computer Science, Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y en Sistemas (IIMAS), Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Coyoacán, Mexico
Keywords: Deep learning;electroencephalogram;emotion recognition;eye tracking;multi-modal domain adaptation
Abstract: Multi-modal classifiers for emotion recognition have become prominent, as the emotional states of subjects can be more comprehensively inferred from Electroencephalogram (EEG) signals and eye movements. However, existing classifiers experience a decrease in performance due to the distribution shift when applied to new users. Unsupervised domain adaptation (UDA) emerges as a solution to address the distribution shift between subjects by learning a shared latent feature space. Nevertheless, most UDA approaches focus on a single modality, while existing multi-modal approaches do not consider that fine-grained structures should also be explicitly aligned and the learned feature space must be discriminative. In this paper, we propose Coarse and Fine-grained Distribution Alignment with Correlated and Separable Features (CFDA-CSF), which performs a coarse alignment over the global feature space, and a fine-grained alignment between modalities from each domain distribution. At the same time, the model learns intra-domain correlated features, while a separable feature space is encouraged on new subjects. We conduct an extensive experimental study across the available sessions on three public datasets for multi-modal emotion recognition: SEED, SEED-IV, and SEED-V. Our proposal effectively improves the recognition performance in every session, achieving an average accuracy of 93.05%, 85.87% and 91.20% for SEED; 85.72%, 89.60%, and 86.88% for SEED-IV; and 88.49%, 91.37% and 91.57% for SEED-V.

Titulo: In-Silico Trained AI for Enhanced T2 Spectrum Imaging and Myelin Water Fraction Mapping in Preclinical 7T MRI
Autor_norm: D. Vallejo-Aldana; A. Ramírez-Manzanares; E. J. Canales-Rodríguez; T. Yu; A. Cisneros-Mejorado; L. Concha; J. Rafael-Patiño; J. -P. Thiran
Afiliacion1: Computer Science Department, Centro de Investigación en Matemáticas A.C, Guanajuato, México; Computer Science Department, Centro de Investigación en Matemáticas A.C, Guanajuato, México; Signal Processing Laboratory (LTS5), École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL), Lausanne, Switzerland; Signal Processing Laboratory (LTS5), École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL), Lausanne, Switzerland; Instituto de Neurobiología, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Juriquilla, México; Instituto de Neurobiología, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Juriquilla, México; Signal Processing Laboratory (LTS5), École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL), Lausanne, Switzerland; Signal Processing Laboratory (LTS5), École Polytechnique Fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL), Lausanne, Switzerland
Keywords: In-silico;Machine learning;Brain;MRI;Myelin water imaging;Pre-clinical data.
Abstract: This study introduces a Machine Learning (ML) approach for estimating the T2 spectrum and myelin water fraction (MWF) using multi-echo T2 (MET2) data from preclinical 7T Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) scanners. ML methods have shown promise in MWF estimation, outperforming Regularized Non-Negative Least Squares (RNNLS). However, existing ML methods were optimized for high signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) typical of 3T clinical MET2 data with larger voxel sizes. We adapted the Model-Informed Machine Learning (MIML) method to handle challenges in preclinical 7T MRI, including reduced voxel sizes, elevated noise levels (SNR=30-60), and shifts in T2 lobes. Results from in-silico simulated data demonstrate the superior performance of the proposed multi-layer-perceptron-based solution over RNNLS. Validation with ME T2 data from two mice—a healthy control and a cuprizone-exposed pathological mouse—confirms the ML method’s success in identifying cuprizone-induced demyelination. Our study showcases the adaptability and enhanced performance of the MIML approach under challenging preclinical 7T MRI conditions, contributing to the advancement of MWF estimation methods in high-field MRI settings.

Titulo: Comparative Analysis of Evolutionary Operators in NSGA-II for Beam Angle Selection in Intensity Modulated Radiotherapy
Autor_norm: N. Ojeda-Ortega; M. Moyano; C. Hernández; S. González-Gallardo; G. Cabrera-Guerrero
Afiliacion1: Escuela de Ingeniería Informática, Pontificia Universidad Católica de Valparaíso, Valparaíso, Chile; Departamento de Ingeniería Industrial, Universidad Católica del Norte, Antofagasta, Chile; Instituto de Investigaciones en Matemáticas Aplicadas y Sistemas, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Ciudad de México, México; Departamento de economía Aplicada (Matemáticas), Universidad de Málaga, Málaga, España; Escuela de Ingeniería Informática, Pontificia Universidad Católica de Valparaíso, Valparaíso, Chile
Keywords: Beam angle optimisation;Multiobjective optimisation;Evolutionary algorithm;NSGA-II;Intensity Modulated Radiation Therapy
Abstract: Beam angle optimisation in intensity-modulated radiotherapy is inherently multi-objective, requiring a balance between target coverage and the sparing of organs at risk. Although evolutionary approaches exist in the literature, their application in multi-objective contexts remains limited. Genetic algorithms have proved effective in single-objective variants of the problem, making it relevant to extend and analyse their components in multi-objective settings. This work examinates the impact of different genetic operators within an NSGA-II-based multi-objective evolutionary algorithm to solve the multi-objective beam angle optimisation problem. Specifically, we evaluate four crossover operators; Simulated Binary Crossover (SBX),Partially Matched Crossover (PMX), Random Union Crossover (RUX), and One-Point Crossover (OX1); combined with two mutation strategies: random replacement and add/subtract mutation. Experimental results show that operator choice significantly affects the convergence and diversity of the Pareto front. Among the combinations analysed, RUX-A/S and RUX-R achieve the best performance in terms of hypervolume and IGD. These findings underscore the importance of operator design when adapting evolutionary algorithms to the multi-objective BAO problem.

Titulo: Incorporation of Deep Learning Regularization in the Image Reconstruction Algorithm for a Dual-Panel PEM Scanner
Autor_norm: F. G. Moncada-Gutiérrez; M. Rodríguez-Villafuerte; H. Alva-Sánchez; A. Martínez-Dávalos
Afiliacion1: CDMX, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico; CDMX, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico; CDMX, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico; CDMX, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico
Keywords: Image reconstruction;Deep learning;Positrons;Physics;Plasmas;Nuclear medicine;Image quality;Distortion;Training data;Single photon emission computed tomography
Abstract: A dual-panel Positron Emission Mammography (PEM) scanner prototype is under development in our research group, and its imaging performance is currently being evaluated. The main challenge for dual-panel scanners is the presence of limited-angle artifacts, which appear in the images due to the acquisition geometry. These artifacts diminish the quality of the images, degrade the contrast of the lesions, and generate distortions in the cross-plane slices (perpendicular to the panels). To mitigate such distortions, deep learning regularization has been incorporated into the List Mode Ordered Subsets Expectation Maximization (LM-OSEM) image reconstruction algorithm. For the regularization step, a U-Net was trained with Monte-Carlo simulated data for several image quality phantoms. Data augmentation strategies were used to increase the diversity of the training data and to construct a model that generalizes more effectively to unseen data. The deep learning model was trained with 3840 pairs of cross-plane images of a dual-panel PEM scanner as input and corresponding slices from a dedicated breast-PET ring scanner as target. This approach was selected because a ring-shaped scanner does not face the limited-angle problem. The Forward-backward splitting expectation maximization (FBSEM) algorithm was implemented with the trained U-Net. This framework was applied to both MC-simulated and experimentally acquired data. In both cases, the quality of the cross-plane images was enhanced, without hampering the in-plane slices (parallel to the panels), and the enhancement in the quality of the images was quantified by image quality metrics like noise, contrast, and the Mean Absolute Error (MAE).

Titulo: Mitigating Limited Angle Artifacts in a Dual-Panel Positron Emission Mammography Scanner Using Deep Learning Regularization
Autor_norm: F. G. Moncada-Gutiérrez; M. Rodríguez-Villafuerte; H. Alva-Sánchez; A. Martínez-Dávalos
Afiliacion1: National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico City, Mexico; National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico City, Mexico; National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico City, Mexico; National Autonomous University of Mexico, Physics Institute, Mexico City, Mexico
Keywords: Deep learning;Training;Microwave integrated circuits;Sensitivity;Monte Carlo methods;Semiconductor detectors;Positrons
Abstract: A dual-panel Positron Emission Mammography (PEM) scanner is a dedicated breast imaging device with higher spatial resolution and enhanced sensitivity, in comparison with conventional Positron Emission Tomography (PET) scanners. However, one of the main challenges this scanner faces are the limited angle artifacts that arise in the cross-planes. To mitigate such artifacts, deep learning regularization has been included in the List Mode Ordered Subsets Expectation Maximization (LM-OSEM) reconstruction algorithm. Regularization has been applied using a U-Net trained with Monte-Carlo simulated data. Network training was achieved using slices of cross-plane images of a PEM scanner as input and corresponding slices from a dedicated breast-PET ring scanner as target, since this scanner does not have the limited angle problem. In the regularization stage, the trained U-Net is employed to process the cross-plane slices of the preceding image estimation. Subsequently, the resultant output is fused with the former image estimate utilizing Forward-backward splitting expectation maximization (FBSEM). This image reconstruction framework has demonstrated a notable enhancement in image quality when assessed from the cross-plane perspective, while maintaining the quality of images when assessed from the in-plane perspective. With this approach, PEM images may be effectively analyzed as three-dimensional images.

Titulo: Large-Scale Validation and Analysis of the Rejection Capacity in Entropic Associative Memory
Autor_norm: Á. F. Bórquez; R. Morales; L. A. Pineda
Afiliacion1: Universidad de Sonora, Guadalajara, Jalisco, México; Universidad de Guadalajara, Guadalajara, Jalisco, México; Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Guadalajara, Jalisco, México
Keywords: Entropic Associative Memory;Quick Draw;scalability;open-set recognition;rejection
Abstract: The Entropic Associative Memory (EAM) is a cognitively inspired computational model that combines probabilistic recognition with an efficient pattern storage and retrieval architecture. Previous evaluations, limited to small domains of about ten classes and a few thousand instances per class, showed promising results in classification and reconstruction. This work extends those studies in two ways. First, we assess scalability using Googles Quick, Draw! dataset, which includes over 300 classes and 100,000 instances per class. Second, we test EAMs rejection capability by storing only half of the classes and evaluating performance on the full dataset. Results confirm EAMs adaptability to large-scale scenarios and its effective rejection of novel stimuli, underscoring its potential as a robust and explainable AI model.
